APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The paper addresses an NLP task (automated short-answer scoring) and uses standard ML evaluation practices, so the topic is within an NLP/ML conference’s scope. However, the contribution is primarily an application of Random Forests with surface and cosine-similarity features on a very small, item-limited dataset (8 items, ~70–120 responses each) and emphasizes a human-in-the-loop support system rather than novel modeling advances. Given the limited data, lack of broader benchmarking or novel methodological contributions, the work feels borderline for a top-tier NLP/ML venue and may be more appropriate for an educational/assessment or applied NLP workshop.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The experiments provide a reasonable real-world trial (8 test items from the Gakken nationwide 2015 exam with sample sizes ~70–120) and report sensible metrics (exact match, within-one, OOB error) and modeling details (Random Forest, 500 trees), and the paper explicitly discusses failures and limitations (entailment difficulty, class imbalance, ineffective case-grammar features). However, no ablation studies or sensitivity analyses are reported, code is not mentioned (limiting reproducibility despite some hyperparameter detail), and the experimental scope is narrow (only 8 items, limited comparisons to alternative models). Related work coverage is described as adequate, but the missing analyses and limited dataset justify a mid-level rating.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper mainly applies existing techniques (Random Forests instead of SVMs, surface-fit features, and cosine semantic similarity) to Japanese short-answer scoring, with a pragmatic addition of mandatory content-word checks. Automated short-answer scoring is a well-studied problem, and the work’s reliance on keyword/semantic-similarity features (not deeper entailment) and evaluation on a small 8-item dataset indicate mostly an incremental, application-focused contribution rather than a genuinely new method. Overall, it combines known techniques with limited novelty.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is generally well-organized and follows a clear flow from methodology (Random Forests, predictors, hyperparameters) through datasets and evaluation metrics to limitations and conclusions, with main contributions stated early (automated Japanese short-answer scoring system, support features). Notation and experimental details appear consistent and sufficiently reported (e.g., RandomForest in R, 500 trees, hyperparameters reported), and limitations are explicitly discussed. Writing quality is described as adequate and related work coverage is adequate, though a few item-specific performance exceptions (e.g., World History B2 #3, Japanese History B1 #2) suggest some presentation of results could be clarified further.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper's main claim—that a Random Forest based system using surface and semantic-similarity features can produce automated short-answer scores that agree with human raters within one point for ~70–90% of cases when deep semantic judgment is not required—is supported by experiments on the Gakken trial data (8 items, ~70–120 responses each) and by reporting of model setup (Random Forests, 500 trees) and OOB error. However, the experimental methodology is limited: per-item sample sizes are small and highly imbalanced (many zeros), there is no broader baseline comparison or statistical significance testing, and reliance on internal OOB estimates rather than an independent test set weakens claims about generalization. The authors acknowledge key limitations (difficulty with textual entailment, poor performance on some items such as Japanese History B1 #2), so the conclusions are cautiously justified but additional data, stronger baselines, and statistical validation are needed to fully support the claims.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper only compares its system directly to human ratings (identical and within-one-point agreement) rather than to prior automated scoring methods; although it mentions using Random Forests rather than SVMs, no empirical baseline experiments with existing algorithms are reported. The human comparison is fair (same data and metrics), and related work coverage is described as adequate, but the lack of experimental comparisons to established automated baselines (e.g., SVM-based or other short-answer scoring systems) means important baselines are missing. Therefore the comparisons are partial but not comprehensive.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem—automating Japanese short-answer scoring for high-stakes national-style exams—and demonstrates a usable human-in-the-loop prototype with Random Forests and semantic-similarity features that achieved ~70–90% within-one-point agreement on many items. However, the evaluation is limited to 8 items with small/imbalanced samples (~70–120 each) and the approach struggles with deep textual entailment and some item types, which constrains immediate wide adoption. Given these promising but limited results, the work is likely to be a useful, incremental contribution that practitioners may build on but is not transformative for the field.

main_claims: We developed an automated Japanese short-answer scoring and scoring-support system for new National Center written test exams (prototype web system on Linux).

The system uses Random Forests with many predictors including surface-fit features and semantic (cosine) similarity between student answers, model answers, and test-item sentences rather than SVMs, to produce multi-level score classifications.

On a trial using 8 social-studies test items from the Gakken nation-wide trial examination (fiscal year 2015), the automated scores agreed with human scores within one point for about 70–90% of cases when high semantic judgement was not required (exceptions exist, e.g., World History B2 #3).
The system supports specification of mandatory content words/phrases (e.g., "father's feeling") to enforce scoring criteria, and is intended to entrust final judgment to human raters.
_summary.methodology: Collect human-scored short-answer data and train Random Forest classifiers (500 trees using the RandomForest package in R) with predictors capturing surface-fit measures and semantic (cosine) similarity between student answers, model answers, and test-item sentences; produce automated score recommendations with prediction probabilities and present them to human raters for revision.
datasets: Gakken nation-wide trial examination (fiscal year 2015) — 8 test items across World History, Japanese History, and Geography (sample sizes ~70–120)
metrics: identical-prediction rate (percentage of exact matches to human scores)
probability within one point (percentage where automated vs human score differ by ≤1)
error rate estimated internally by Random Forests (out-of-bag)
limitations: Recognizing textual entailment / deep semantic equivalence between model and student answers remains difficult; system relies primarily on keyword/semantic similarity rather than full entailment.
Agreement of case elements (case-grammar based features) was almost never observed and was not an effective predictor.
Insufficient and imbalanced human-labeled score data (many answers scored zero) limits supervised learning effectiveness, producing poor performance for some items (e.g., Japanese History B1 #2).
The system is intended as a support tool and requires human raters for final judgments.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - automated short-answer scoring / educational assessment
